shai-hulud.description: Self-replicating npm worm. Compromised maintainer accounts via phishing, published malicious versions of widely-used packages, exfiltrated GitHub/npm tokens, installed a persistence daemon with a destructive failsafe.
shai-hulud.failsafe_warning: DO NOT revoke npm/GitHub tokens until the host is isolated from the network. The gh-token-monitor daemon detects revocation and runs rm -rf ~/.

shai-hulud-2.description: Second wave of the Shai-Hulud campaign. Re-emerged via newly compromised maintainer accounts; tooling refined, persistence patterns updated.
shai-hulud-2.failsafe_warning: Same as Shai-Hulud 1.0 — DO NOT revoke tokens until isolated.

chalk-sept-2025.description: Phished maintainer credentials used to publish a malicious chalk update; postinstall exfiltrated env vars.

sandworm-mode.description: Campaign targeting maintainers via spear-phishing; published packages with an obfuscated postinstall identified by the constant SANDWORM_MODE in the bundle.

axios-mar-2026.description: Brief malicious version of axios published; postinstall script exfiltrated environment variables via DNS to attacker-controlled domains.

mini-shai-hulud-tanstack.description: Self-spreading worm variant targeting TanStack ecosystem maintainers via compromised contributor accounts. Reduced footprint vs Shai-Hulud 1.0/2.0 — no persistence daemon observed.

mcp-heuristics.description: Heuristic patterns for AI-agent MCP server configurations that are suspicious by structure (not by attribution). Each indicator here flags a pattern that warrants verification, not a confirmed-malicious server. See docs/MCP-IOC-GUIDE.md for context and how to contribute confirmed-malicious entries.

file.description: Shai-Hulud LaunchAgent persistence plist on macOS. CRITICAL: this also implies the destructive failsafe is armed.

what_to_do: DO NOT revoke npm or GitHub tokens yet — the failsafe daemon watches for revocation
Disconnect this machine from the network immediately
From an isolated terminal: `launchctl unload <plist-path>` then delete the plist file
Only after isolation: rotate tokens (npm, GitHub) and audit account activity

process.description: Shai-Hulud token-monitor daemon running. Armed destructive failsafe — see docs/SHAI-HULUD-FAILSAFE.md before any defensive action.

what_to_do: DO NOT revoke tokens — see file indicator PZ-shai-hulud-file-001
Isolate the machine from the network, then kill the process and remove persistence

github.description: Shai-Hulud uses stolen GitHub credentials to create repositories under the victim's account, often named with recognizable patterns.

what_to_do: Verify you did NOT create the matching repo(s)
If unfamiliar, archive or delete the repo from the GitHub web UI (do not use the gh CLI on the affected machine)

mcp.description: Typosquat of the official @modelcontextprotocol/* scope. Names resembling the official scope but published from a different scope are a known attack vector for MCP supply-chain compromises.

what_to_do: Verify the MCP server package is from the legitimate @modelcontextprotocol/ scope (no plural, no extra letters, no helper/tools suffix)
Cross-check the official server list at https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers
If the typosquat package was actually run by your agent: rotate any credentials the server had access to and audit recent agent sessions

mcp.description: MCP server configured to communicate over plaintext HTTP. There is no legitimate reason for a remote MCP endpoint to use http:// instead of https://; the traffic is tamperable in transit.

what_to_do: Switch the configured URL to https:// if the server supports it
If the server does not support TLS, remove it from your MCP config — there is no good reason to expose an agent to a tamperable transport
Audit recent agent sessions for unexpected tool calls or data movement

mcp.description: MCP server configuration passes a sensitive credential (GITHUB_TOKEN, NPM_TOKEN, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY) in its env block. This can be intentional and legitimate (e.g. a GitHub MCP server requires a token), but it means a compromised version of the server has direct access to that credential. Verify the server is trusted.

what_to_do: Confirm you trust the MCP server package source and version that is receiving this credential
Prefer scoped tokens with minimum-required permissions (e.g. read-only) over full-access tokens
Watch for unexpected use of the credential in your provider's audit log
